graph.name: Snapchat Architecture - Sending and Receiving Snaps
graph.notes: Architecture for Snapchat's core functionality, enabling users to send and receive ephemeral media (Snaps) at massive scale (300M+ DAU, 5B+ Snaps/day, 10M+ QPS). Key design decisions include building custom layers (SnapDB) on top of AWS services for specific features like transactions, TTL, and incremental state synchronization to optimize for latency (24% P50 reduction in snap send latency) and cost. Leverages Graviton instances and auto-scaling within EKS clusters to manage high compute footprint (900+ EKS clusters, many with 1000+ instances). DynamoDB stores 400TB of metadata and undergoes nightly scans of 2 billion rows per minute for friend suggestions and ephemeral data deletion.

nodes.name: iOS App
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Used by 300+ million daily active users (DAU).

nodes.name: Android App
nodes.notes: WORKLOAD_PEEK: Used by 300+ million daily active users (DAU).

nodes.name: EKS Gateway
nodes.notes: An EKS service built to front-end communication from mobile apps. WORKLOAD_PEEK: Part of 900+ EKS clusters, many with 1000+ instances. Uses auto-scaling and Graviton instances for cost optimization. P50 latency of sending a snap reduced by 24% after moving to this architecture.

nodes.name: EKS Media Delivery Service
nodes.notes: An EKS service responsible for handling snap media delivery, sending snaps to CloudFront and persisting in S3. WORKLOAD_PEEK: Part of 900+ EKS clusters, many with 1000+ instances.

nodes.name: CloudFront CDN
nodes.notes: Used to cache and deliver snap media closer to recipients, reducing latency. Serves media for both sending (from) and receiving (to) users.

nodes.name: S3 Object Storage
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Stores the actual snap media (images, videos, chat information) after being pushed through CloudFront.

nodes.name: EKS SnapDB
nodes.notes: A custom database layer built on top of DynamoDB to provide higher-level features like transactions, TTL, efficient ephemeral data handling, and incremental state synchronization. This reduces burden on DynamoDB and controls costs. Stores metadata associated with snaps.

nodes.name: DynamoDB Metadata Store
nodes.notes: DATA_PEEK: Stores 400 terabytes of metadata for Snaps and user relationships. WORKLOAD_PEEK: Nightly scans run 2 billion rows per minute for friend suggestions and deleting ephemeral data. Heavy usage.

nodes.name: EKS Messaging Service
nodes.notes: Responsible for pushing messages to clients, highly latency-sensitive. Looks up connection IDs from ElastiCache. WORKLOAD_PEEK: Part of 900+ EKS clusters, many with 1000+ instances.

nodes.name: ElastiCache Connection Metadata
nodes.notes: Stores connection IDs and server IDs to quickly find the persistent connection a server has to a client for low-latency message delivery (receiving snaps).

nodes.name: EKS Friend Graph
nodes.notes: Microservice running on EKS. Used for managing friend relationships and suggestions. WORKLOAD_PEEK: Performs nightly scans on DynamoDB.

edges.notes: User initiates sending a snap via the iOS app.
User initiates sending a snap via the Android app.
Gateway talks to the media delivery service.
Sends snap media to CloudFront for distribution.
CloudFront persists snap media in S3 for storage.
SnapDB stores snap metadata in DynamoDB.
Performs nightly scans (2 billion rows per minute) on DynamoDB for friend suggestions and ephemeral data deletion.
Looks up connection ID and server ID for the recipient client.
Returns connection metadata (connection ID, server ID).
Sends a message to the recipient via the EKS Gateway.
Pushes the message to the iOS client.
Pushes the message to the Android client.
iOS client retrieves snap media using a media ID from CloudFront.
Android client retrieves snap media using a media ID from CloudFront.
CloudFront retrieves snap media from S3.
